region_of_origin: South America (widely cultivated across Asia and Singapore)
food_subgroup: Legumes and nuts — raw or roasted peanuts

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side serving of peanuts in a Singapore hawker or noodle context is approximately 30 g, equivalent to a small handful or a small condiment bowl.

nutrient_source.title: Peanuts, all types, raw — USDA FoodData Central, SR Legacy, FDC ID 174263
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw peanuts, all types (FDC ID 174263). USDA SR Legacy is an approved secondary global source under Project Veritas source policy. Raw peanut values are used as the nutritional baseline for this record. Roasting causes only minor changes to macronutrient composition and the SR Legacy raw entry is the most defensible primary anchor available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; peanuts GI=14 (glucose reference)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Peanuts have a well-established GI of 14 (glucose reference=100) as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and published in the peer-reviewed 2008 International Tables of GI and GL values (Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2008;88:1793-1820). Glycemic load per 30 g serving calculated as: (GI 14 × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100. Available carbohydrate per 30 g = (16.13 g total carbohydrate − 8.5 g dietary fibre) × 0.30 = 2.289 g. GL = (14 × 2.289) / 100 = 0.32.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content; rich in potassium and magnesium
hypertension.guidance: Peanuts are naturally low in sodium (18 mg per 100 g) and provide potassium (705 mg) and magnesium (168 mg), both of which support healthy blood pressure; individuals with hypertension should choose unsalted peanuts and limit portion size to avoid excess caloric intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI 14); high protein and fibre slow glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Peanuts have a very low GI of 14 and a negligible glycemic load per serving, making them a suitable snack for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; their high protein (25.8 g) and dietary fibre (8.5 g) content further attenuate postprandial glucose response, though caloric density warrants portion control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High in monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats; zero cholesterol; rich in niacin and vitamin E
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Peanuts are rich in heart-healthy monounsaturated fats (24.4 g per 100 g) and polyunsaturated fats (15.6 g), contain zero cholesterol, and provide niacin and vitamin E, which are associated with improved lipid profiles; moderate consumption as part of a balanced diet is consistent with cardiovascular health guidelines, though their high energy density requires portion awareness.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peanuts (花生, Kacang tanah, வேர்க்கடலை, मूंगफली) are the edible seeds of the legume Arachis hypogaea, widely consumed across Singapore and Southeast Asia as a snack, condiment, and ingredient. Despite being classified botanically as a legume, peanuts are nutritionally and culinarily treated as a nut. In the Singapore hawker context, peanuts are commonly served as a side accompaniment to braised noodle dishes such as Hock Kee Braised Beef Noodles, either roasted, boiled, or lightly salted. Raw peanuts have a mild, earthy flavour with a firm, slightly waxy texture; roasting intensifies their nutty aroma and produces a crunchy texture. Nutritionally, peanuts are energy-dense (567 kcal per 100 g) and provide substantial protein (25.8 g), healthy unsaturated fats (predominantly oleic acid), dietary fibre (8.5 g), folate (240 mcg DFE), niacin (12.07 mg), vitamin E (8.33 mg), magnesium (168 mg), and potassium (705 mg). Their very low glycemic index (GI 14) makes them suitable for blood glucose management. Peanuts are a major allergen and must be declared on food labels. They are naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal. Clinically, peanuts are associated with improved lipid profiles, satiety, and glycemic control when consumed in moderate portions.
common_user_queries: What is the GI of peanuts?
Are peanuts good for heart health?
What are peanuts called in Chinese?
Are peanuts a nut or a legume?
What is the Malay name for peanuts?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for raw peanuts (all types, FDC ID 174263), which is an approved secondary global source under Project Veritas source policy. GI value of 14 is well-established in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Am J Clin Nutr 2008). Ontology codes are verified real identifiers. Multilingual names are confirmed standard terms.
notes: trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; peanuts contain no industrially produced trans fats. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as peanuts are a plant food containing no dietary cholesterol. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; raw peanuts contain negligible vitamin C. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as peanuts are a plant food; vitamin B12 is not synthesised by plants. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; peanuts do not contain vitamin D. The field survey listing price of SGD 3.96 is preserved from the input record for reference only and is not used as a nutritional data point. The serving context is a small side portion (~30 g) accompanying braised beef noodles at Hock Kee Braised Beef Noodles, Lorong Telok, Singapore. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI 14 × available carbohydrate per 30 g serving 2.29 g) / 100 = 0.32. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value 24.43 reported by USDA SR Legacy). polyunsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value 15.56 reported by USDA SR Legacy).
